title: James Cameron Rejects AI Actors in Hainan: 'I Wouldn't Do It'
text:
Dec 9, 2025 5:58am PT
James Cameron Rejects AI Actors at Hainan Festival: ‘I Wouldn’t Do It. Is It Desirable?’
By
Marcus Lim
Plus Icon
Marcus Lim
Latest
Hong Kong FilMart 2026: Six Takeaways From Asia’s Buzzy Film and Entertainment Content Marketplace
2 months ago

2 months ago

Getty Images
James Cameron
came out swinging against AI-generated performers in Hainan, saying he “wouldn’t do it” and openly doubting whether replacing actors with algorithms is “desirable” at all.
Speaking at a packed masterclass at the seventh
Hainan Island International Film Festival
(HIIFF), the “
Avatar: Fire and Ash
” director weighed in firmly on the side of flesh-and-blood talent, in a debate which — together with the future of theatrical releases — has energized much of Hollywood this year.
Related Stories
Oscar-Nominated Inga Ibsdotter Lilleaas to Star in ‘The Outsider,’ With Wagner Moura (EXCLUSIVE)
Asghar Farhadi Blows Kisses as French Drama 'Parallel Tales,' Starring Isabelle Huppert, Scores 5.5-Minute Cannes Ovation
“I’m not personally interested in using those tools, in using any pathway that uses technology to replace human creativity,” said Cameron.
Popular on Variety
“We may be able to replace an actor [with a generative character]. I say ‘we’, but I wouldn’t do it. Is it desirable? Does it create that unique character that is based on two sets of unique human experiences, the screenwriter’s and the actor’s?”
Cameron conceded that generative AI and large language models had their use cases within the creative industries, while sounding caution at how they were used.
“Can we improve the workflow, can we make the work more efficient, can we make the work more creative [with generative AI]? I think yes. As long as we stick to a very high standard of how it is used, ethically, morally and practically,” said the “Terminator 2” director.
However, he sounded a note of caution about the limitations of the technology in combating basic issues facing the industry.
“If you try to use a model that’s trained on everything, how can it be that unique? It cannot. It will give you the average. It can do the mediocre, but it cannot do the special and unique. It also cannot create that which has never been seen.”
“If you take a generative model and say ‘give me something that looks like “Avatar”?’ Great! it can do it all day long, bio luminescence, flying creatures,” said Cameron.
“Ask that model to do it before ‘Avatar’ existed. It’s going to draw a blank. So, it fundamentally still comes back to human creativity.”
Cameron also weighed in on the need for substantial theatrical windows, a topic fresh on Hollywood minds with Paramount promising extended theatrical releases in a fresh takeover bid of Warner Bros. Discovery.
“It’s very important right now. If we love cinema, we probably love the theatrical experience. Streaming has taken up a lot of what used to be a theater experience, because it gives you immediacy and immediate access,” Cameron said.
He went on to decry the psychological impact of short-form content on audiences and their attention spans.
“We are distracted by a lot of short form media, and even with streaming we can make everything a short-form video with our remote. Everything is coming in a fragmented, short attention span mode. How do we counter that? By going to the movies.”
Cameron continued: “You’ve imposed upon yourself, by your own choice, a focus and an engagement with what is happening on that screen. It can be a much more profound, much deeper experience. When a jet fighter pilot trains to fight in combat, they train in simulators. Movies are simulators for things that might happen in our lives, that can prepare ourselves mentally for joy, love, tragedy, loss, death, all the human experiences.”
Cameron was at the HIIFF for the China premiere of “Avatar: Fire and Ash.” Attendees included the film’s star, Zoe Saldaña, as well as Zhang Ziyi, who provides the Chinese voice performance of the character of Ronal, played by Kate Winslet.
Actor Michelle Yeoh, who has filmed scenes for “Avatar 4,” turned up in the audience of the gala presentation, which also doubled up as a showcase of China Film Group’s CINITY led panel-based large screen system. Unlike typical cinema projection systems, led panel systems are able to present high frame rate 3D with greater brightness and deeper colour and black contrast.
